═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

scToolkit package

  • `sc_code` — source Core analysis logic — preprocessing, filtering, clustering, and automated downstream visualizations. Serves as the main entry point for running standardized workflows with robust defaults and integrated error handling.

  • `sc_config` — source Centralized config and parameter handling for all analysis steps. Stores key workflow settings (e.g., dataset name, paths, clustering options) in a standard dictionary structure attached to adata.uns[“config”]. This enables consistent parameter tracking, reproducibility, and versioning without relying on external schema validation libraries.

  • `sc_plots` — source Unified plotting interface for UMAPs, dot plots, violins, heatmaps, ridline, density. Designed for consistency, with shared color handling and auto-labeling across all major marker and enrichment visualizations.

  • `sc_utils` — source Extensive collection of general-purpose utilities for data inspection, stat collection, GPU setup, file system interactions, and result formatting. Includes helpers for safe dictionary access, object copying, marker parsing, random sampling, and unified path management. Central to avoiding duplication and ensuring consistent behavior across the entire codebase.

  • `utils` — source Low-level helper functions — array handling, parsing, normalization, and error-safe lookups.

  • `common_logger` — source Unified logging — structured terminal output, verbosity control, and consistent formatting across CLI and notebook environments.

  • `sc_legacy` — source Deprecated config logic and compatibility layers used to migrate early versions of the framework to the modern, validated config structure.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

sc_code

  • `sc_code` — source Core analysis logic — preprocessing, filtering, clustering, and automated downstream visualizations. Serves as the main entry point for running standardized workflows with robust defaults and integrated error handling.

    See the sc_code.run_downstream() Guide for the run_downstream output explaination.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════

Here you see the major functions to call for a pre defined single cell experiment.

Core API for executing standardized single-cell RNA-seq analysis workflows using Scanpy. This module wraps preprocessing, filtering, clustering, and visualization steps in a modular and reproducible fashion.

Key Concepts:

  • setup_adata(adata, **config_kwargs): Initializes adata with configuration, stats, and GPU setup. This replaces manual calls to sc_config.get_config(...) and associated setup logic.

  • adata.uns[“config”]:

    Stores all analysis parameters (e.g., dataset name, paths, clustering settings) as a centralized config object created via sc_config. Used throughout the pipeline for consistent behavior.

  • adata.uns[“stats”]:

    Contains dataset-level summary statistics (e.g., gene/cell counts, QC metrics) computed during initialization. Serves as a reference for filtering and diagnostics.

  • adata.uns[“genesets”]:

    A dictionary where keys are gene set names and values are lists of genes. Used for running gene set enrichment methods across the dataset.

  • adata.uns[“ref_dict”] / [“ref_dict_upd”] / [“ref_dict_upd_subs”]:

    Contain reference gene set definitions and their filtered or updated versions used for enrichment benchmarking and comparison.

  • Conventions for Function/Method arguments:

    • key: Single reference to adata.obs, adata.obsm, adata.var_names, etc. Usually the doc specifies, which of them are usable.

    • keys: List or dict of keys, grouped logically.

    • ref: Mostly dict, but also sometimes list for group to gene/markers mapping. This can be genes, or whatever modality you have the features. This is different from the keys because it is not related to the obs, only to the adata.var_names. DON’T confuse this with reference

    • ref_dict: Like ref, but specific for dictionarys mapping group to fetures.

    • ref_df: Like ref, but specific for a datafame.

    • obs_key: A single column in adata.obs.

    • obs_keys: A list of columns in adata.obs.

    • groupby: Categorical column in adata.obs, used for grouping.

    • reference: Used for comparisons, there reference marks the reference, or for the general term refernce sort according to reference. THIS has nothing to do with ref.

  • Conventions for Function/Method name prefixes:

    • plot_: plotting function start with it usually they have saving and are not to confuse with show_ which are checkers mostly.

    • print_: Printing helpers for too long lines or dataframes in chunks etc.

    • get_: getters start.

    • extract_: Usually reserved for subsetters or refiners instead of get.

    • calc_: calculator start.

    • search_: Package or database search helpers .

    • check_: Checkers, to for validation or quick checks.

    • ref_: Handling helpers for ref like remove genes not present in adata.

    • df_: Dataframe handling helpers e.g for conversion to ref_dict.

    • update_: Updating functions for config or nested structures

    • replace_: For functions that stack replace things.

    • filter_ or tag_: For functions that filter or tag (Mostly QC)

    • Possibly more and if you find a function, that is not according to conventions, please report it.

Typical Workflow:

import scanpy as sc
from scToolkit import sc_code
from scToolkit.sc_utils import initialize_cuda

adata = sc.read_h5ad("your_file.h5ad")
sc_code.setup_adata(
    adata,
    param_setup="default",
    dataset_name="your_dataset",
    save_path="results/",
    use_GPU=True)
sc_code.all_qc_preprocessing(adata)
sc_code.plot_filtering(adata, before=True)
adata = sc_code.all_filters_tagging(adata, apply_filters=True)
sc_code.plot_filtering(adata, before=False)
adata = sc_code.Run_all_prep_steps_clustering(adata)
adata = cluster_SC_scanpy_like(adata)
adata = run_downstream(adata)

Summary:

This module supports execution of all steps required for a full single-cell workflow. It is tightly coupled with sc_config.py for dynamic config handling and expects adata.uns["config"] to be initialized via setup_adata.

This Module provides the general functions to call for a single cell experiment.

General conventions for the package:

key: usually can be one of adata.obsm.keys(), adata.obs.keys, adata.var_names (sometimes also the adata.varm.keys and adata.obs_names)

keys: like key but a list or dict with key as groups.

obs_key: A single key in the adata.obs.keys() obs_keys: a list of adata.obs.keys()

group is the general synonyme for categorical columns cluster/celltype/ 2 different arguments arrise groupby: therefore must be a single key in adata.obs.keys() and categorical (adata.obs[groupby].dtype.name == “category”)

══════════════════════════════════════════════════

scToolkit.sc_code.Run_all_prep_steps_clustering(adata: AnnData, filter: bool = True) → AnnData

Run the preprocessing steps for gene expression data.

This function performs a series of preprocessing steps on the provided adata object, including filtering genes, normalization, log-transforming, scaling, and regression. The steps are configurable based on the settings in adata.uns["config"]. It also handles saving intermediate results in adata.layers and updating the preprocessing status in adata.uns["stats"]["preprocessors_ran"].

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • filter (bool, optional) – If True, performs exclusion of MT/Ribos/Pseudo genes. Defaults to True.

Returns:

Updated adata object with the applied preprocessing steps.

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Raises:

NotImplementedError – Raised if regression on CPU is attempted, as it is not implemented.

Calls:

calc_highly_variable_genes_unique_based, filter_genes_n_families, filter_genes_n_families_regex, get_shape_diff, hist_equalize_data, mark_highly_variable_genes

Tags:

config, normalization, pipeline, scaling, stats

scToolkit.sc_code.all_filters(adata: AnnData, prot: bool = False, post_filter: bool = True) → AnnData

Runs all filtering criteria defined in adata.uns["config"]["pp"][keys].

The function filters cells, genes, and quality control variables in an adata object based on the configuration provided in adata.uns["config"]["pp"]. The keys used for filtering are filter_cells, filter_genes, and filter_qc_var.

Note

Deprecated, use the all_filters_tagging, it doesn’t rely on the slow scanpy functions anymore.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object that contains the dataset to be filtered.

  • prot (bool, optional) – Specifies if Citeseq data is included. Defaults to False.

  • post_filter (bool, optional) – Filters out empty cells and rows after general filtration. Defaults to True.

Returns:

The filtered adata object. The function does not deepcopy the object, and the filtering is done inplace.

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Calls:

filter_cells_multicall, filter_genes_multicall, get_shape_diff

Tags:

QC, config, obs, stats, var

scToolkit.sc_code.all_filters_tagging(adata: AnnData, qc_key: str = 'good_quality', reset: bool = False, return_filter_log: bool = False, apply_filters: bool = False) → DataFrame | tuple[AnnData, DataFrame] | AnnData | None

Runs all filtering criteria defined in adata.uns["config"]["pp"][keys].

The filtering keys include: filter_cells, filter_genes, and

filter_qc_var.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • qc_key (str, optional) – Key in adata.obs and adata.var to mark good quality cells/genes. Default is “good_quality”. Defaults to “good_quality”.

  • reset (bool, optional) – If True, resets the qc_key values before applying new filters. Default is False. Defaults to False.

  • return_filter_log (bool, optional) – If True, returns a DataFrame with filter effect summary. Default is False. Defaults to False.

  • apply_filters (bool, optional) – If True, returns a adata object with filter effect summary. Default is False. Defaults to False.

Returns:

  • If return_filter_log is True and apply_filters is False:

    Returns a pandas.DataFrame with filter statistics.

  • If both return_filter_log and apply_filters are True:

    Returns a tuple (filtered AnnData, filter log DataFrame).

  • If only apply_filters is True:

    Returns filtered anndata.AnnData.

  • Otherwise:

    Returns None.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame | tuple[anndata.AnnData, pandas.DataFrame] | anndata.AnnData | None

Raises:

  • KeyError – If the required keys are not present in adata.uns["config"]["pp"].

  • Warning – If specific keys in the filtering criteria are not present in adata.obs or adata.var.

Called By:

all_qc_preprocessing

Tags:

QC, config, obs, pipeline, stats, var

scToolkit.sc_code.all_qc_preprocessing(adata: AnnData, part: str = '/qc/', prot: bool = False, try_rerun: bool = False, apply_filters: bool = False) → None

This function annotates the quality control (QC) genes and calculates the QC variables.

This function annotates QC-related genes and calculates various QC metrics. It does not utilize a GPU due to the overhead outweighing the performance gains. Additionally, it focuses on percentages rather than raw counts for QC measures and removes the raw layer from the adata object, as it does not provide additional utility and can interfere with certain functionalities.

Note

  1. This function doesn’t use a GPU by now, because the overhead of

    moving the data to a GPU is larger than the speed benefit of computing on the GPU.

  2. The percentage of major genes is removed, and also the actual counts

    of the QC measure, because we are only interested in percentages. (may change, because the actual counts can be informative for duplicates?!?)

  3. This function deletes the raw layer, because it has no use and

    interferes with some functionality.

The default created obs keys are:

  • n_genes: Number of genes a cell has.

  • n_counts: Total counts of a cell.

  • n_unique: Number of unique count values a cell/gene has.

  • n_unique_score: Scored uniqueness, considering the number of unique values and the uniqueness of each value. (Experimental)

  • pct_MT: Percentage of mitochondrial content.

  • pct_RBS: Percentage of ribosomal content.

  • pct_MTRBS: Percentage of mitochondrial ribosome content. (Deprecated, has no information??)

  • pct_RBS_MTRBS: pct_RBS + pct_MTRBS (Deprecated, has no information??)

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • part (str, optional) – Path to save the generated figures. Defaults to “/qc/”.

  • prot (bool, optional) – Indicates if the data is Citeseq data. Defaults to False.

  • apply_filters (bool, optional) – If true, it applys the filters directly. Defaults to False.

Returns:

None

Calls:

all_filters_tagging, convert_to_mem_efficient, flag_gene_family, get_n_unique, setup_image_folder

Tags:

QC, config, obs, pipeline, stats, var

scToolkit.sc_code.cluster_SC_scanpy_like(adata: AnnData, gene_subeset: list[str] = [], run_pca: bool = True, run_neighbors: bool = True, run_clustering: bool = True, run_umap: bool = True, hierarchical_clustering: bool = True) → AnnData

Runs PCA, neighborhood graph, unsupervised clustering (leiden), and UMAP on an adata object.

This function is designed to perform a sequence of preprocessing and clustering steps on single-cell RNA sequencing data stored in an adata object.

It includes the following steps:

  • Principal Component Analysis (PCA),

  • neighborhood graph construction,

  • clustering using the Leiden algorithm,

  • Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) for visualization.

Note

Use adata = cluster_SC_scanpy_like(adata) because this function operates in-place, but it might updates the symlink for GPU computation.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • gene_subeset (list[str], optional) – Gene set to run the PCA on. If not set, the function uses adata.uns["highly"]. Defaults to [].

  • run_pca (bool, optional) – Whether to (re-)calculate PCA. Defaults to True.

  • run_neighbors (bool, optional) – Whether to (re-)calculate neighbors. Defaults to True.

  • run_clustering (bool, optional) – Whether to (re-)calculate unsupervised clustering. Defaults to True.

  • run_umap (bool, optional) – Whether to (re-)calculate UMAP. Defaults to True.

  • hierarchical_clustering (bool, optional) – If a post hierarchical clustering of the clusters should be performed. Defaults to True.

Returns:

Returns the updated adata object with all the performed computations.

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Raises:

  • AttributeError – If an invalid algorithm is specified for sc.pp.neighbors.

  • AttributeError – If an outdated or unsupported clustering algorithm is chosen.

Calls:

get_group_hierarchy, get_highly_variable, set_neighbors_n_pcs

Called By:

search_for_leiden_resolution, search_for_neighbor_params

Tags:

clustering, config, pipeline, visualization

scToolkit.sc_code.collect_n_pcs_for_variance_sums(adata: AnnData, pca_variance_sums: list[float] | tuple[float, ...] = [0.9, 0.925, 0.95, 0.975, 0.985, 0.99], to_plot: bool = False) → list[int] | tuple[int, ...]

Determine the number of principal components (PCs) for given variance thresholds.

For each variance sum in pca_variance_sums, this function updates the PCA configuration in adata.uns and applies sc_advanced.set_neighbors_n_pcs. This helper is assumed to update adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["neighbors"]["n_pcs"] to reflect the number of PCs required to reach the specified variance.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object with expected structure in adata.uns["config"].

  • pca_variance_sums (list[float] | tuple[float, optional) – List or tuple of cumulative variance thresholds for PCA. Defaults to [0.90, 0.925, 0.95, 0.975, 0.985, 0.99].

  • to_plot (bool, optional) – Whether to plot the variance explained during PCA selection. Passed to set_neighbors_n_pcs. Defaults to False.

Returns:

list[int] or tuple[int, …]: Number of PCs selected for each

threshold. Returned in the same order as pca_variance_sums.

Return type:

list[int] | tuple[int, …]

Raises:

  • KeyError – If required config keys are missing from adata.uns.

  • AttributeError – If sc_advanced.set_neighbors_n_pcs fails to update adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["neighbors"]["n_pcs"].

Calls:

set_neighbors_n_pcs

Called By:

search_for_neighbor_params

Tags:

calculation, config

scToolkit.sc_code.downstream_preprocessing(adata: AnnData, ref_dict: dict = {}, calc_deg: bool = True, score_genes: bool = False, deg_key: str = 'rank_genes_groups', use_whyever_default_scanpy_deg: bool = False, layer: str | None = 'log2norm_counts') → AnnData

Run downstream preprocessing for a single modality.

Note

This function will overwrite the X with layer!

This function handles the downstream preprocessing for a single modality of the provided adata object. It includes various steps such as calculating highly variable genes, updating marker gene dictionaries, scoring cell types cluster-wise, and optionally ranking gene groups.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object containing the data to be processed.

  • ref_dict (dict, optional) – Dictionary of reference marker genes. Defaults to {}.

  • calc_deg (bool, optional) – If True, calculates the DEGs (rank_genes_groups). Defaults to True.

  • score_genes (bool, optional) – If True, scores genes based on predefined criteria. Defaults to False.

  • deg_key (str, optional) – Key for the DEG results stored in adata.uns. Defaults to “rank_genes_groups”.

  • "rank_genes_groups". (Defaults to)

  • layer (str | None, optional) – The layer to compute the DEGs for. Defaults to “log2norm_counts”.

Returns:

The processed adata object with updated attributes.

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Raises:

KeyError – If required keys are not found in the adata object.

Calls:

calc_DEGs, get_all_markers_from_ref_dict, get_highly_variable, get_valide_ref_dicts, ref_dict_sort_values_hvg_like, score_genes

Called By:

run_downstream

Tags:

DEG, annotation, config, pipeline

scToolkit.sc_code.get_receptor_ligand_db(organism: str = 'human', adata: AnnData | None = None, subset_var_key: str | None = None) → DataFrame

Load receptor–ligand interactions from OmniPath.

Parameters:

  • organism (str, optional) – Organism to use. Only "human" is supported. Defaults to "human".

  • adata (ad.AnnData, optional) – AnnData object for subsetting interactions to genes present in adata.var_names. Defaults to None.

  • subset_var_key (str, optional) – .var key to further restrict to a subset of genes (e.g. "highly_variable"). Defaults to None.

Returns:

Filtered receptor–ligand interactions.

Return type:

pd.DataFrame

Raises:

  • ValueError – If the organism is not available in the mapping.

  • FileNotFoundError – If the OmniPath database file is missing.

scToolkit.sc_code.get_ref_db(db: str = 'Combined', databases: list[str] | None = None, ref_dict: dict[str, list[str]] | None = None, all_celltypes_to_use: list[str] | None = None, organism: str = 'human') → DataFrame

Retrieves marker data from various databases for specific cell types in a specified organism.

This function interfaces with multiple databases (e.g., PanglaoDB, CellTypist, CellMarker, SCType) to retrieve markers (genes) associated with specific cell types. Depending on the specified database, it filters and processes the data to return a formatted DataFrame. It supports customization through several parameters, including organism type and cell types of interest.

Note

  • organism=”mouse” only: The database of Decouplr is “well” curated and the mouse genes only consist of capital letters, we convert them to first capital and rest lower.

  • FILTER CELLS example for plasma cells: cell_types = sc_utils.search_database(get_ref_db(), “plasma”) # CHECK the each of the celltypes in cell_types, all of them will be # considered for gene extraction subsetted_df = combined_database[combined_database[“cell_type”].isin(cell_types)] sc_utils.get_element_and_counts_with_positional_sum(subsetted_df)[:n_genes] # more examples at the doku of search_database)

  • We cleaned some of the databases, e.G having non Capitalized Genes for the human [‘PanglaoDB’, ‘CellMarker’, ‘SCType’], and cleaned spaces in the SCType

Parameters:

  • db (str, optional) – The name of the database to retrieve data from. Default is “PanglaoDB”. Defaults to “Combined”.

  • databases (list[str] | None, optional) – A list of database names to use when db is set to “Combined”. Default is None. Defaults to None.

  • ref_dict (dict[str, optional) – A dictionary containing custom markers to include. Default is None.

  • all_celltypes_to_use (list[str] | None, optional) – A list of cell types to filter the results by. Default is None. Defaults to None.

  • organism (str, optional) – The organism for which to retrieve marker data. Must be “human” or “mouse”. Default is “human”. Defaults to “human”.

Returns:

A DataFrame containing the marker data filtered and formatted based on the specified parameters.

Return type:

pandas.DataFrame

Raises:

ValueError – If the specified organism is not “human” or “mouse”.

Calls:

df_split_col, get_ref_db

Called By:

get_ref_db

scToolkit.sc_code.hist_equalize_data(adata: AnnData, bins: int = 50, mask: ndarray | None = None, use_cell_only: bool = True) → AnnData

DO NOT USE THIS FUNCTION!!! It is (was) under development.

Runs histogram equalization for each cell in the adata object, rescaling the cells to have an evenly spaced distribution instead of a negative-binomial distribution.

This function is designed to adjust the data within each cell, or optionally across all cells, to achieve a more uniform distribution. This may enhance clustering results by summarizing similar expressed values into one value, but it is not suitable for differential expression analysis (DEG).

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • bins (int, optional) – The number of bins to use for spacing. More bins result in stronger discretization. Defaults to 50.

  • mask (numpy.ndarray | None, optional) – A mask indicating which elements to include. Defaults to None.

  • use_cell_only (bool, optional) – Whether to treat each cell separately during equalization. Defaults to True.

Returns:

The adata object with histogram equalized data.

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Calls:

StatKeeper.get

Called By:

Run_all_prep_steps_clustering

Tags:

normalization, scaling

scToolkit.sc_code.load_h5ad(path_to_h5ad: str) → AnnData

Loads an adata object from an H5AD file and populates its .uns attribute with experiment configuration data from a corresponding pickle file.

Note

The function assumes that the pickle file containing experiment configuration data has the same base name as the H5AD file, but with the appropriate compression extension (e.g. .pickle.xz, .pickle.gz, or .pickle).

Parameters:

path_to_h5ad (str) – Path to the H5AD file to be loaded.

Returns:

The loaded adata object with experiment configuration data added to adata.uns["config"].

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Raises:

FileNotFoundError – If the corresponding pickle file is not found in the same directory as the H5AD file.

Called By:

load_spatial_prox

Tags:

config, io, pipeline

scToolkit.sc_code.run_decoupler(adata: AnnData, ref: dict[str, list[str]] | DataFrame, run_type: str = 'consensus', weight: str | None = None, methods: list[str] | None = None, verbose: bool = False, min_n: int = 5, layer: str = 'log2norm_counts', use_raw: bool = False) → None

Runs the Decoupler tool to analyze and visualize cell type-specific marker expression.

The function applies various decoupling methods on single-cell data provided in adata, using predefined ref/markers and the specified run type. It allows for customization options like adjusting for specific layers.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • ref (dict[str] | pandas.DataFrame) – Marker genes to use in the decoupling analysis.

  • run_type (str, optional) – The type of decoupling analysis to run. Defaults to “consensus”.

  • visualize (bool, optional) – Whether to generate visualizations. Defaults to False.

  • weight (str | None, optional) – Weight column name in the ref DataFrame. Defaults to None.

  • methods (list[str] | None, optional) – List of decoupling methods to use. Defaults to None.

  • databases (str, optional) – Path to databases used in the analysis. Defaults to None.

  • verbose (bool, optional) – Whether to output verbose information. Defaults to False.

  • min_n (int, optional) – Minimum number of cells required for the analysis. Defaults to 5.

  • layer (str, optional) – Layer in adata to use instead of the default expression matrix. Defaults to “log2norm_counts”.

  • use_raw (bool, optional) – Whether to use the raw adata. Defaults to False.

Returns:

The function updates adata in-place with decoupling results and possibly visualizations.

Return type:

None

Raises:

NotImplementedError – If an unsupported run_type is provided.

Calls:

run_viper

Tags:

annotation, config

scToolkit.sc_code.run_downstream(adata: AnnData, ref_dict: dict = {}, part: str = '/downstream/', rerun_DEG: bool = False, deg_key: str = 'rank_genes_groups', with_figure_explaination: bool = True, disable_sc_verbosity: bool = True, layer: str | None = 'log2norm_counts') → AnnData

Run the DEG calculations and plot/save all desired visualisations.

The DEGs are calculated based on the adata.uns[“config”][“general”][“cluster_algorithm”] classes. The plots are shown/saved based on the adata.uns[“config”][“to_plot”]. The DEG and stat CSVs are created base on adata.uns[“config”][“to_create”]

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object post clustering.

  • ref_dict (dict, optional) – Reference dictionary for plotting and DEG calculation. Defaults to {}.

  • part (str, optional) – Path to save the output. Defaults to “/downstream/”.

  • rerun_DEG (bool, optional) – Flag to rerun DEG calculations. Defaults to False.

  • deg_key (str, optional) – Key for the DEG results stored in adata.uns. Defaults to “rank_genes_groups”.

  • with_figure_explaination (bool, optional) – Include figure explanations in logs. Defaults to True.

  • disable_sc_verbosity (bool, optional) – Disable scanpy verbosity. Defaults to True.

  • layer (str | None, optional) – The layer to compute the DEGs for. Defaults to “log2norm_counts”.

Returns:

The processed adata object.

Return type:

anndata.AnnData

Calls:

continuos_umap_helper, create_group_stats_df, discrete_umap_helper, downstream_preprocessing, get_DEG_gene_csvs, get_DEGs_per_group, get_adata_by_mod, get_colormap, get_highly_variable, get_print_highlighter, get_save_path, plot_embedding_density, plot_mediods_heatmap, plot_per_group_DEG_dotplot_n_gene_dendrogram, plot_per_group_DEG_umaps, plot_per_group_stacked_violins, plot_ref_dotplots, plot_ref_stacked_violins, plot_umap, save_genesets_to_csv, setup_image_folder

Tags:

DEG, annotation, config, pipeline, visualization

scToolkit.sc_code.run_pca_on_subset(adata: AnnData, config: dict | None = None) → None

Runs PCA on a subset of the adata object based on selected genes and adds the PCA results to the original adata object.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object to be modified in-place.

  • config (dict | None, optional) – Configuration dictionary containing PCA parameters. Defaults to None.

Returns:

None (modifies adata in place)

Return type:

None

Tags:

clustering, config

scToolkit.sc_code.save_h5ad(adata: AnnData, path_to_h5ad: str, compression: str = 'gzip', save_umap: bool = True, force_remove: bool = True) → None

Saves an adata object with experiment_dict.

Note

  • If interrupted, adata.uns["config"] might not be restored properly.

  • JSON is avoided due to HDF5 limitations with None values and lists/arrays.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object with experiment_dict in adata.uns["config"].

  • path_to_h5ad (str) – Path to save the adata object.

  • compression (str, optional) – The compression to use for h5ad. Defaults to “gzip”.

  • save_umap (bool, optional) – Whether to save the UMAP object. Defaults to True.

  • force_remove (bool, optional) – If True, deletes any existing file before saving. Defaults to True.

Returns:

This function performs an in-place save operation and returns nothing.

Return type:

None

Raises:

  • ValueError – If there is an issue with saving the adata object.

  • KeyboardInterrupt – If the operation is interrupted by the user.

Calls:

convert_mixed_types

Called By:

Spatial_prox.save

Tags:

config, io, pipeline

scToolkit.sc_code.search_for_leiden_resolution(adata: AnnData, resolutions: list[float] | None = None, n_eval: int = 30, keys: list[str] = [], jaccard_paired_keys: list[list[str, str]] = [], n_cluster_range: list[int] | None = None, return_clusterings: bool = True, dpi: int = 60, figsize: Sequence[float | int] = [6, 5], plot_densitys: bool = True, umap_kwargs: dict = {}, density_kwargs: dict = {}, jaccard_kwargs: dict = {}) → None

Visualize different Leiden clusterings optionally with marker genes.

This function evaluates different Leiden clustering resolutions on an adata object and optionally visualizes the clustering results with specified marker genes using UMAP.

Note

  • The n_eval parameter is not used if resolutions is provided.

  • Ensure that the adata object is properly pre-processed before passing it to this function.

  • If you break the execution of this function, it will overwrite the config! Better to save the object before.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object containing the data to cluster.

  • resolutions (list[float] | None, optional) – List of resolutions to evaluate for Leiden clustering. If not provided, a default range of 30 resolutions from 0.01 to 1.5 will be used. Defaults to None.

  • n_eval (int, optional) – Number of resolutions to sample if resolutions is not provided. Defaults to 30.

  • 30. (Defaults to)

  • keys (list[str], optional) – List of marker genes to plot on the UMAP. If provided, these ref/markers will be highlighted in the UMAP visualization. Defaults to [].

  • jaccard_paired_keys (list[list[str, str]]) – List of paired observation keys from adata.obs to compare. Each list must contain two valid column names in adata.obs. The function computes Jaccard similarity between the categories defined by those paired keys.

  • n_cluster_range (list[int] | None, optional) – [min, max] allowed number of clusters. Defaults to None.

  • return_clusterings (bool, optional) – If True, return a dict of clustering Series per resolution. Defaults to True.

  • dpi (int, optional) – DPI parameter for the plots, lots of plots might overflow the memory. Defaults to 60.

  • figsize (Sequence[float | int], optional) – figsize parameter for the plots, lots of plots might overflow the memory. Defaults to [6.

  • plot_densitys (bool, optional) – If True, the categorical columns will be plotted as density plot. Defaults to True.

  • umap_kwargs (dict) – Extra keyword arguments passed to plot_umap. Defaults to {}.

  • density_kwargs (dict) – Extra keyword arguments passed to plot_embedding_density. Defaults to {}.

  • jaccard_kwargs (dict) – Extra keyword arguments passed to plot_jaccard_heatmap_cluster_comparison. Defaults to {}.

Returns:

The function modifies the adata object in-place and produces UMAP visualizations.

Return type:

None

Calls:

cluster_SC_scanpy_like, plot_umap_cat_splitting

Tags:

clustering, config, visualization

scToolkit.sc_code.search_for_neighbor_params(adata: AnnData, n_pcs: list[int] | None = None, n_neighborss: list[int] | None = None, min_dists: list[float] | None = None, keys: list[str] = [], jaccard_paired_keys: list[list[str, str]] = [], clean_keys: bool = False, return_umaps: bool = True, dpi: int = 60, figsize: Sequence[float | int] = [6, 5], plot_densitys: bool = True, umap_kwargs: dict = {}, density_kwargs: dict = {}, jaccard_kwargs: dict = {}) → dict | None

Visualize different UMAPs optimized with marker genes.

This function evaluates different UMAP parameters (min_dist and n_neighbors) to visualize their effects on the clustering of an adata object. It allows users to plot UMAPs with different configurations while optionally highlighting specific marker genes.

Note

  • Ensure that the adata object is properly pre-processed before passing it to this function.

  • If you break the execution of this function, it can possibly overwrite or delete the config! Better to copy the object before.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • n_pcs (list[int] | None, optional) – A list of n_pcs values to evaluate for the Neighborhood. Defaults to None.

  • n_neighborss (list[int] | None, optional) – A list of n_neighbors values to evaluate for UMAP. Defaults to a list generated by linspace between 5 and 30 with 6 steps. Defaults to None.

  • min_dists (list[float] | None, optional) – A list of min_dist values to evaluate for UMAP. 25, 0.5, 0.75]. Defaults to None.

  • keys (list[str], optional) – A list of marker genes to plot on the UMAP. Defaults to [].

  • jaccard_paired_keys (list[list[str, str]]) – List of paired observation keys from adata.obs to compare. Each list must contain two valid column names in adata.obs. The function computes Jaccard similarity between the categories defined by those paired keys.

  • clean_keys (bool, optional) – If True, filters the marker genes to those present in the adata object. Defaults to False.

  • dpi (int, optional) – DPI parameter for the plots, lots of plots might overflow the memory. Defaults to 60.

  • figsize (list[int] | tuple[int], optional) – figsize parameter for the plots, lots of plots might overflow the memory. Defaults to [6.

  • plot_densitys (bool, optional) – If True, the categorical columns will be plotted as density plot. Defaults to True.

  • umap_kwargs (dict) – Extra keyword arguments passed to plot_umap. Defaults to {}.

  • density_kwargs (dict) – Extra keyword arguments passed to plot_embedding_density. Defaults to {}.

  • jaccard_kwargs (dict) – Extra keyword arguments passed to plot_jaccard_heatmap_cluster_comparison. Defaults to {}.

Returns:

dict or None: Nested dictionary of UMAP coordinates if return_umaps=True, else None.

Return type:

dict | None

Calls:

cluster_SC_scanpy_like, collect_n_pcs_for_variance_sums, plot_umap_cat_splitting

Tags:

clustering, config, visualization

scToolkit.sc_code.set_neighbors_n_pcs(adata: AnnData, ratio_key: str = 'variance_ratio', variance_sum: float | None = None, use_automatic_pca_variance_sum: str | None = None, evaluate_at_pcs: int | None = None, overwrite_config: bool = True, to_plot: bool = True) → int | None

Automatically estimates the number of principal components (PCs) to use for the neighborhood graph based on explained variance.

This function selects the number of PCs by applying either a cumulative sum threshold (“cumsum”) or a cumulative difference heuristic (“cumsum_diffs”) on PCA variance values. The strategy and threshold can be configured via adata.uns["config"].

Note

If your data is as noisy as scRNA-seq (and you don’t have computation power), it may make sense to use this. But if you want to do a proper analysis, use the “cumsum” method.

This function overwrites the adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["neighbors"]["n_pcs"] parameter based on the setting in adata.uns["config"]["general"]["use_automatic_pca_variance_sum"] which can be either “cumsum” or “cumsum_diffs”.

For many samples, genes may be all quite variable. Consider using the parameter setting >>> adata.uns["config"]["general"]["use_automatic_pca_variance_sum"] = "cumsum_diffs" >>> adata.uns["config"]["general"]["pca_variance_sum"] = 0.95 (or 0.99 if the number of PCs would be below 10).

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object with PCA results stored in adata.uns["pca"] and config under adata.uns["config"].

  • ratio_key (str, optional) – Key for explained variance ratio array in adata.uns["pca"]. Defaults to “variance_ratio”.

  • variance_sum (float | None, optional) – Desired cumulative variance threshold. If None, uses value from config["general"]["pca_variance_sum"]. Defaults to None.

  • use_automatic_pca_variance_sum (str | None, optional) – Method to compute PC count is “cumsum” or “cumsum_diffs”. If None, uses config["general"]["use_automatic_pca_variance_sum"]. Defaults to None.

  • evaluate_at_pcs (int | None, optional) – If set, logs cumulative variance at the specified PC index. Defaults to None.

  • overwrite_config (bool, optional) – Whether to write result into config["pp"]["neighbors"]["n_pcs"]. If False, returns the PC count. Defaults to True.

  • to_plot (bool, optional) – If True, plots PCA variance and chosen threshold. Useful for debugging. Defaults to True.

Returns:

None or int: Updates adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["neighbors"]["n_pcs"] in place by default. If overwrite_config is False, returns the selected number of PCs as an integer.

Return type:

int | None

Raises:

KeyError – If required keys are missing in adata.uns["pca"] or adata.uns["config"].

Calls:

StatKeeper.get

Called By:

cluster_SC_scanpy_like, collect_n_pcs_for_variance_sums

Tags:

clustering, config, visualization

scToolkit.sc_code.setup_adata(adata: AnnData, overwrite: bool = False, **config_kwargs) → None

Prepares the adata object for downstream analysis by configuring and initializing necessary settings.

This function adds configurations, statistical summaries, and GPU initialization to the adata object to prepare it for further processing. If configurations or stats are already present in adata, they will only be overwritten if overwrite is set to True.

Note

If the GPU initialization fails, you may need to rerun this function with use_GPU=False and overwrite=True to bypass GPU setup.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object to be configured and prepared for analysis.

  • overwrite (bool, optional) – If True, existing configurations and stats in adata will be overwritten. Defaults to False.

  • **config_kwargs – Additional configuration parameters passed to sc_config.get_config().

Returns:

None

Raises:

  • KeyboardInterrupt – Raised if GPU initialization is interrupted manually.

  • Exception – Raised if GPU initialization fails due to any other issue.

Calls:

get_config, get_stats, initialize_cuda

Tags:

config, pipeline, stats

scToolkit.sc_code.test_logger() → None

Helper

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

sc_config

  • `sc_config` — source Centralized config and parameter handling for all analysis steps. Stores key workflow settings (e.g., dataset name, paths, clustering options) in a standard dictionary structure attached to adata.uns[“config”]. This enables consistent parameter tracking, reproducibility, and versioning without relying on external schema validation libraries.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════

Module: configuration_setup

The Config Object for Pipeline Execution of scanpy.

This module provides the functionality to configure and set up an experiment_dict object for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) experiments. The get_config function in this module is designed to generate a comprehensive configuration dictionary with predefined sections for various steps of the scRNA-seq analysis pipeline, including quality control, data scaling, normalization, clustering, and downstream analysis.

The configuration generated by this module is highly customizable and can be adapted to specific experimental setups and analysis requirements. It allows for fine-tuning of parameters related to data processing, gene selection, plotting options, and more. Additionally, it provides support for both CPU and GPU processing, enabling efficient handling of large-scale scRNA-seq datasets.

What to plot and what to save is handled by adata.uns[“config”][“to_plot”] and adata.uns[“config”][“to_create”]. The documentation for that will be available someday hopefully.

Key Features:

  • Quality Control: Configurable options for mitochondrial, ribosomal, and pseudogene filtering, with organism-specific presets for human and mouse.

  • Data Scaling and Normalization: Options to save and process different data layers, including counts, log-transformed data, and scaled data.

  • Clustering and Downstream Analysis: Comprehensive setup for unsupervised clustering using methods like Leiden, with support for visualizing UMAPs, heatmaps, and dot plots.

  • Plotting Options: Extensive configuration for generating various plots, including violin plots, scatter plots, dendrograms, and more, with the ability to customize color maps and plot aesthetics.

  • GPU Support: Enables GPU acceleration for clustering and UMAP calculation, improving performance on large datasets.

Usage Notes:

  • Users are encouraged to review and adjust the configuration settings to match their specific experimental needs. Critical settings such as save paths, data layers to save, and organism type should be carefully set.

  • NOTE: Some features and options in the configuration are marked as experimental or deprecated and should be used with caution. For instance, scaling and histogram equalization settings may require additional validation before use in analysis.

  • TODO: The module contains placeholders for future enhancements, such as support for additional organisms and more general keys for all analyses. Users contributing to the codebase should implement these features as needed.

Dependencies:

  • This module relies on external libraries, including Scanpy for scRNA-seq analysis and Matplotlib for plotting. Ensure all necessary packages are installed and up-to-date.

  • The module also references external paths for database files and gene sets. These paths should be correctly set up and accessible in the user’s environment.

TODO: The module contains placeholders for future enhancements, such as support for

additional organisms and more general keys for all analyses. Users contributing to the codebase should implement these features as needed.

  • If some collaborations require always the same setup, you can define it here:
    1. Create a function that adjusts the config:
      def param_your_colab_partners(config: experiment_dict) -> experiment_dict:

      config[“whatever_to_change”] = “whatever” return config

    2. Run the function conditionally in get_config:
      if param_setup == “your_colab_partners”:

      config = param_your_colab_partners(config)

Examples: Here are a variety of examples illustrating how to modify the configuration for a Scanpy single-cell analysis. These examples cover common adjustments as well as more advanced customizations.

>>> # Don't plot the leiden and sample umap
>>> adata.uns["config"]["to_plot"]["down"]["visualize_leiden_umap"] = False
>>> # Plot the Quality measures on the umap
>>> adata.uns["config"]["to_plot"]["down"]["visualize_qc_umap"] = True
>>> # save the DEGs as csvs
>>> config["to_create"]["down"]["DEG_csv"]["create"] = True
>>> # Don't save the clustering stats as csvs
>>> config["to_create"]["down"]["cluster_stats_csv"]["create"] = False

Please add the config object to the adata.uns[“config”]. This small example load the library, create a default object and save it to the adata object.

>>> # Load the library
>>> from scToolkit import sc_config, sc_utils
>>> # Enable cuda support for the code
>>> sc_utils.initialize_cuda()
>>> # Create the config object
>>> config = sc_config.get_config(
...     param_setup="default",
...     dataset_name="your_dataset_or_experiment_name",
...     save_path="path_to_the_analysis_output",
...     use_GPU=True)
>>> # add the config object to the adata
>>> adata.uns["config"]

If you want to change the standard parameters of any scanpy function you can change it like e.g.

>>> # Change the minimum genes to filter cells
>>> adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["filter_cells"]["min_genes"] = 500
>>> # Change the maximum Mitochondrial content to filter cells
>>> adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["filter_qc_var_obs"]["max_pct_MT"] = 8
>>> # Change the QC key to plot violins
>>> adata.uns["config"]["general"]["keys_for_qc"]["obs"] = ["pct_MT", ...]
>>> adata.uns["config"]["general"]["keys_for_qc"]["var"] = ["n_cells", ...]

The config object also contains function, which are not h5ad/json serializable! Therefore use the scToolkit saving and loading functions please!

Examples::
NOTE:

  • Here we will use config which is synonymus for adata.uns[“config”]

  • BUT keep in mind config = adata.uns[“config”] is a symbolic link if you change config, it will also change the adata.uns[“config”].

1.1. Save the adata and the config:

>>> # no compression is faster, but more disk space required
>>> sc_utils.save_h5ad(adata, "path/to/adata.h5ad", compression=None)
>>> # Load the adata and the config
>>> adata = sc_utils.load_h5ad("path/to/adata.h5ad")

1.2. Enable GPU acceleration for neighbors computation:

>>> # Set automatically everything to GPU usage
>>> config = get_config(use_GPU=True)
>>> # All methods support manual changing from change_gpu_usage():
>>> # #########################################################
>>> # Set general GPU usage flag in the configuration
>>> adata.uns["config"]["general"]["use_GPU"] = "True" if enable else "False"
>>> # #########################################################
>>> # Configure GPU acceleration for individual components
>>> # ##########################################
>>> # Configure PCA computation method
>>> if "pca" in to_enable:
...     adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["pca"]["method"] = "rapids" if enable else "pca"
>>> # ##########################################
>>> # Configure Leiden clustering method
>>> if "leiden" in to_enable:
...     adata.uns["config"]["tl"]["umap"]["method"] = "rapids" if enable else "umap"
>>> # ##########################################
>>> # Configure nearest neighbors computation method
>>> if "neighbors" in to_enable:
...     adata.uns["config"]["tl"]["leiden"]["method"] = "rapids" if enable else "leiden"
>>> # ##########################################
>>> # Configure UMAP embedding method
>>> if "umap" in to_enable:
...     adata.uns["config"]["pp"]["neighbors"]["transformer"] = "rapids" if enable else "sklearn"

1.3. Update the save path and enable overwriting existing results:

>>> # Set the save path
>>> config = get_config(save_path="/path/to/save", overwrite=True)
>>> # change the save path
>>> # DON'T do: config["general"]["save_path"] = ... it won't create the folder!
>>> # USE:
>>> sc_config.set_save_path("/path/to/save", overwrite=True)

1.4. Increase the number of cores used for parallel processing:

>>> # This is unfortunately is only for the scanpy methods that support parallelization
>>> # basically the ones we don't advise you to use: draw_graph, tsne, regress_out
>>> config = get_config(n_cores=40)
>>> # To set the generall number of threads used by python
>>> utils.set_threads(40)
>>> # NOTE: A pc is doing many things aside, dont use 40 if you only have 40 cores,
>>> #       leave some cores for the system

1.5. Set the random seed for reproducibility across all steps (non GPU only!):

config["general"]["seed"] = 12345  # Default is 42
1.6. Set the organism type to “mouse” and adjust QC gene identifiers:

Manually setting is not necessary, due to automation, but you can add new regex.

NOTE:

THIS MUST HAPPEN AFTER YOU run the setup_adata and before sc_code.all_qc_preprocessing(adata)

>>> config = setup_adata(organism="mouse")
>>> config["qc"]["gene_identifiers"][config["general"]["mitochondiral_key"]] = "^mt-"
>>> config["qc"]["gene_identifiers"][config["general"]["your_genes"]] = "^Cd"
>>> sc_code.all_qc_preprocessing(adata)
1.6.2. Specify a custom set of mitochondrial genes for QC filtering in Regex (^ for start | for or $ for end):
NOTE:

THIS MUST HAPPEN AFTER YOU run the setup_adata and before sc_code.all_qc_preprocessing(adata)

>>> config["qc"]["gene_identifiers"]["MT"] = "$|^".join(["MT-ND1", "MT-ND2", "MT-CO1"])
>>> sc_code.all_qc_preprocessing(adata)

1.7. Enable/disable saving of data in the layers:

>>> config["general"]["save_counts"] = True
>>> config["general"]["save_norm_counts"] = True
>>> config["general"]["save_normlog2_counts"] = True
>>> config["general"]["save_regress_out"] = False  # NO ARBITRARY DATA RESCALING!!!!
>>> # NOTE: Scaled is the same as z-score transformation, good for plotting dotplots or heatmaps
>>> config["general"]["save_scaled"] = True
1.8. Disable/Enable the saving of intermediate QC counts data:

Everything is relative. If you want to look for douplets, the pct-MT won’t help but maybe the total_counts_MT can.

>>> config["general"]["del_qc_counts"] = True
1.9. Filter out low-quality cells based on gene count thresholds:

Here you have 2 options, manuall subsetting or with the function.

>>> config["pp"]["filter_cells"]["min_genes"] = 200
>>> config["pp"]["filter_cells"]["max_genes"] = 6000
>>> # ##############################
>>> # Tag the cells
>>> sc_code.all_filters_tagging(adata, reset=True)
>>> # Manual subsetting
>>> adata = adata[adata.obs["good_quality"], adata.var["good_quality"]].copy()
>>> # ##############################
>>> # Tag the cells and subset NOTE: IT RETURNS THE FILTERED OBJECT "adata =" is needed!
>>> adata = sc_code.all_filters_tagging(adata, reset=True, apply_filters=True)
1.10. Exclude specific gene families from analysis using a regular expression:
NOTE:

This must happen before running sc_code.Run_all_prep_steps_clustering(adata) for automatic handling!

>>> config["exclude_gene_family_regex"] = "^MT-|*\.1$"
>>> adata = sc_code.Run_all_prep_steps_clustering(adata)

2.1. Change the method for calculating differentially expressed genes (DEGs):

>>> config["tl"]["rank_genes_groups"]["method"] = "wilcoxon"  # USE "wilcoxon" for good!!!!!!!!!!!!
2.2. Modify the list of highly variable genes by force including marker genes:

Doesn’t acutally make sense, but if Colab partners “Know” which genes are interesting, beeing unbiased is for loosers, who will never publish anything.

>>> config = get_config(use_highly_variable_plus_marker_genes=True, marker_genes=["CD4", "CD8"])
2.3. Customize the violin plot settings for QC analysis:

# This will be used as defaults for all violinplots, unless you use the function argument groupby

>>> # produce the Violin plots in run_downstream
>>> config["to_plot"]["qc"]["all_violins"] = True
>>> config["pl"]["violin"]["groupby"] = "cell_type"

3.1. Change the number of to calculate principal components for PCA:

>>> config["pp"]["pca"]["n_comps"] = 50  # Default is all: min(n_cells, n_genes) - 1

3.2. Enable automatic PCA variance selection based on cumulative differences:

>>> config["general"]["use_automatic_pca_variance_sum"] = "cumsum_diffs"
>>> config["general"]["pca_variance_sum"] = 0.95

3.3. Modify the metrics used in neighbors computation:

>>> config["pp"]["neighbors"]["metric"] = "cosine"  # Default is "euclidean"

3.4. Set a custom number of neighbors to use for neighborhood graph, impacts leiden and UMAP:

>>> config["pp"]["neighbors"]["n_neighbors"] = 15  # Default is 20

3.5. Adjust the UMAP settings to change the minimum distance and spread:

>>> config["tl"]["umap"]["min_dist"] = 0.1  # Default is 0.5
>>> config["tl"]["umap"]["spread"] = 1.5    # Default is 1.0

3.6. Change the clustering resolution for Leiden algorithm:

>>> config["tl"]["leiden"]["resolution"] = 1.0  # Default is 0.75

3.5. Set the key for clustering to use a different method:

>>> config["general"]["cluster_algorithm"] = "leiden"  # There is no alternative!

4.1. Specify a subset of markers to be used for heatmap visualization for run_downstream:

>>> config["to_plot"]["down"]["heatmaps"]["marker_mod"] = ["CD4", "CD8", "GATA3"]

4.2. Set the color map for run_downstram for all heatmap/all in general to “viridis”/”turbo”:

>>> config["pl"]["heatmap"]["cmap"] = "viridis"
>>> # Or overwrite all
>>> sc_config.update_key_in_config(adata_.uns["config"], key_to_update="cmap", new_value="turbo")
>>> # THIS HAS TO BE USED WITH EXTREME CONCIOUSNESS and only used for plotting
>>> # arguments or useless scanpy thing like overwriting "use_raw" = False

4.3. Set the filtering criteria for the creation of a DEGs CSV/xlsx:

>>> # NOTE: An LFC (log2) from 1 to 2 is already 1... quite uninformative as a single measure!
>>> config["to_create"]["down"]["DEG_csv"]["log2fc_min"] = 1.0
>>> # This is actually the adjusted, nobody is interested it the actual pval.
>>> config["to_create"]["down"]["DEG_csv"]["pval_cutoff"] = 0.0001
>>> # percentage of cells that must express the genes in eighter group
>>> config["to_create"]["down"]["DEG_csv"]["pct_min"] = .1
>>> # which table type to save is handled by:
>>> config["general"]["save_df_types"] = [".csv", ".xlsx"]
>>> # available types: '.csv' ,'.xlsx' ,'.json' ,'.html' ,'.feather' ,'.parquet' ,'.pkl' ,'.pickle'

4.4. Adjust the heatmap parameters to visualize the top 50 genes for run_downstream:

>>> config["to_plot"]["down"]["heatmaps"]["DEG_params"]["top_n_genes"] = 50  # Default is 20

4.5. Customize the dendrogram visualization for marker genes:

>>> config["to_plot"]["down"]["marker_dendrograms"]["visualize"] = True
>>> config["to_plot"]["down"]["marker_dendrograms"]["figsize_small"] = [12, 8]  # Default is [10, 7]

4.6. Enable or disable the plotting of UMAP visualizations for run_downstream:

>>> config["to_plot"]["down"]["umap"] = False  # Disable UMAP plotting

4.7. Enable plotting of cluster-specific dot plots for run_downstream:

>>> config["to_plot"]["down"]["cluster_dotplot"]["visualize"] = True

4.8. Define a custom colormap for specific plots:

>>> custom_cmap = ListedColormap(["#FF0000", "#00FF00", "#0000FF"])
>>> config["pl"]["dotplot"]["cmap"] = custom_cmap

4.9. Change the default color palette for cell type annotations for run_downstream:

>>> custom_palette = ["#FF0000", "#00FF00", "#0000FF"]
>>> config["pl"]["scatter"]["palette"] = custom_palette

These examples Illustrate the flexibility of the configuration setup, allowing users to tailor the analysis pipeline to their specific requirements. By adjusting these parameters, you can ensure that the analysis is customized to your experimental needs and preferences. To make full use of this flexibility, it is ESSENTIAL to understand the configuration structure and consult the documentation carefully.

This module is intended for advanced users familiar with scRNA-seq data analysis and Python programming. Proper understanding and careful configuration of the parameters are essential for successful application of the provided functions.

If you something is missing or you want to incorporate different scverse libraries please feel free to contact the single cell Responsibles. (If it is Machine learning related, don’t even ask, it won’t be incorporated, PCA is bad enough already!)

# TODO: add a cell cycle geneset or genesets for each cell cycle phase # TODO: add a function to disable/enable GPU

══════════════════════════════════════════════════ ══════════════════════════════════════════════════

scToolkit.sc_config.change_gpu_usage(adata: object, enable: bool = True, to_enable: list[str] = ['general', 'pca', 'leiden', 'neighbors', 'umap']) → None

Enables or disables GPU support globally or for specific operations in an adata object.

to_enable=[“global”], enable=False, would be global disabling to_enable=[“global”], enable=True, would NOT be global enabling. therefore you have to use to_enable=[“general”, “pca”, “leiden”, “neighbors”, “umap”]

This function modifies the configuration settings in adata.uns to enable or disable GPU acceleration for selected preprocessing and analysis methods, including PCA, Leiden clustering, neighbor graph construction, and UMAP.

Note

This function assumes that adata.uns["config"] follows a specific hierarchical structure with keys for preprocessing (pp) and tools (tl). Ensure that this structure exists before calling this function.

Parameters:

  • adata (object) – adata object containing configuration settings in adata.uns.

  • enable (bool, optional) – Whether to enable (True) or disable (False) GPU support. Defaults to True.

  • to_enable (list[str], optional) – List of operations to modify. Supported options are “pca”, “leiden”, “neighbors”, and “umap”. Defaults to [“pca”, “leiden”, “neighbors”, “umap”].

Returns:

None

Tags:

config

scToolkit.sc_config.dataset_to_dict(path: str, exclude_reference: bool = True, return_regex: bool = False) → dict[str, list[str]]

Creates a CSV of columns of genes to a dictionary.

Note

  • this will exclude the column “reference” because we use it to list the papers to cite for each column!

  • CSV stands for COMMA separeted, no excel or tab or whatever separated!

Parameters:

  • path (str) – Path to the CSV

  • exclude_reference (bool, optional) – If you want to not exclude a colum named “reference”, use at your own risk. Defaults to True.

Returns:

dict[str, list[str]]

Return type:

dict[str, list[str]]

Called By:

get_config

Tags:

annotation, io

class scToolkit.sc_config.experiment_dict

Bases: dict

This class stores the parameter setup for a single-cell experiment.

The experiment_dict class is designed to handle various configuration settings for a single-cell experiment, including quality control (QC) parameters, downstream analysis setups, and plot settings. The dictionary is structured to accommodate different stages of the data processing pipeline, with distinct sections for each major step.

general

A dictionary to store general experiment settings.

Type:

dict

qc

A dictionary to store quality control parameters, including gene identifiers.

Type:

dict

downstream

A dictionary to store downstream analysis configurations.

Type:

dict

scToolkit

A dictionary to store settings related to the Scanpy API.

Type:

dict

to_plot

A nested dictionary to store plotting configurations for different stages.

Type:

dict

to_create

A nested dictionary to store configurations for items to be created, such as DEG CSVs.

Type:

dict

Returns:

None

Tags:

config

set_save_path(save_path: str, overwrite: bool) → None

Sets the save path for the experiment data.

Parameters:

  • save_path (str) – The directory path where the experiment data should be saved.

  • overwrite (bool) – Flag indicating whether to overwrite existing directories.

Raises:

OSError – If the directory cannot be created and does not already exist.

Note

The save_path is trimmed of trailing slashes and updated with a unique identifier if overwrite is set to False and the directory already exists.

sync_dict(dictionary: dict) → None

Synchronizes the current experiment dictionary with another dictionary.

Parameters:

dictionary (dict) – The dictionary to sync with.

Note

DEPRECATED!!! This method resets the save path and calculates non-overlapping keys between the current dictionary and the provided one. The non-overlapping keys are stored under ‘non_overlap’.

scToolkit.sc_config.get_config(param_setup: str = 'default', experiment_name: str = 'default', dataset_name: str = 'default', save_path: str = './analysis/', use_rna: bool = True, use_prot: bool = False, analysis_only: bool = False, overwrite: bool = True, marker_genes: list[str] = [], organism: str = 'human', use_highly_variable_plus_marker_genes: bool = False, use_GPU: bool = True, n_cores: int = 20, selected_citeseq_markers: list[str] = [], selected_gene_markers: list[str] = []) → dict

Generates a configuration dictionary for single-cell analysis.

This function initializes and returns a configuration dictionary for setting up a single-cell analysis pipeline. The configuration includes general processing parameters, dataset-specific settings, quality control settings, clustering preferences, and visualization options.

Note

  • Scaled values are computed as z-score transformations and are useful for dot plots or heatmaps.

  • If use_highly_variable_plus_marker_genes is set, marker genes will be included in the highly variable gene selection.

  • GPU processing is available but starts with clustering.

Parameters:

  • param_setup (str, optional) – Defines the parameter setup name. Defaults to “default”.

  • experiment_name (str, optional) – Name of the experiment. Defaults to “default”.

  • dataset_name (str, optional) – Name of the dataset. Defaults to “default”.

  • save_path (str, optional) – Directory path to save outputs. Defaults to “./analysis/”.

  • use_rna (bool, optional) – Whether to use RNA-based analysis. Default is True. Defaults to True.

  • use_prot (bool, optional) – Whether to include protein-based analysis. Default is False. Defaults to False.

  • analysis_only (bool, optional) – If True, skips all plotting. Default is False. Defaults to False.

  • overwrite (bool, optional) – If True, overwrites existing results. Default is True. Defaults to True.

  • marker_genes (list[str], optional) – List of marker genes to consider. Defaults to [].

  • organism (str, optional) – Specifies the organism (e.g., ‘human’, ‘mouse’). Default is ‘human’. Defaults to “human”.

  • use_highly_variable_plus_marker_genes (bool, optional) – If True, includes marker genes in highly variable genes. Defaults to False.

  • use_GPU (bool, optional) – Whether to use GPU acceleration. Default is True. Defaults to True.

  • n_cores (int, optional) – Number of CPU cores to use. Default is 20. Defaults to 20.

  • selected_citeseq_markers (list[str], optional) – Selected CiteSeq markers. Defaults to [].

  • selected_gene_markers (list[str], optional) – Selected gene markers. Defaults to [].

Returns:

A configuration dictionary for the analysis pipeline.

Return type:

dict

Raises:

NotImplementedError – If an unsupported organism is provided.

Calls:

dataset_to_dict, experiment_dict.set_save_path

Called By:

load_h5ad_old, setup_adata

Tags:

DEG, QC, annotation, clustering, config, normalization, scaling, stats, visualization

scToolkit.sc_config.get_nested_dict_keys_structure(dict_: dict) → dict

Recursively extracts the key structure of a nested dictionary.

Use-Case: For exploring the experiment_dict config like

get_nested_dict_keys_structure(config[“general”])

Parameters:

dict (dict) – The dictionary whose key structure should be extracted.

Returns:

A dictionary reflecting only the key structure of the input.

Return type:

dict

>>> keys_only_dict = get_nested_dict_keys_structure(adata.uns["config"]["general"])
>>> print(keys_only_dict)
Calls:

get_nested_dict_keys_structure

Called By:

get_nested_dict_keys_structure

Tags:

utils

scToolkit.sc_config.get_stats() → dict

Returns a statistics dictionary for saving filtering, gene exclusions and exections.

“preprocessors_ran” is for steps that should not be executed twice.

Returns:

Statistics dictionary for saving in the adata.uns[“stats”]

Return type:

dict

Called By:

setup_adata

Tags:

stats

scToolkit.sc_config.search_regex_nested_dict_list(collection: experiment_dict | dict | list | str, pattern: str, path: str = '', sep: str = '"]["', start: str = '["', end: str = '"]') → None

Recursively searches a dictionary or list for keys or values matching a regex pattern and prints their paths/access.

This function navigates through nested dictionaries or lists, looking for keys or values that match the provided regular expression pattern. When a match is found, the function prints the path leading to that key or value. The path is a string representation showing the hierarchy of keys or indices leading to the matched element.

Note

The function assumes that the data structure is a combination of dictionaries and lists and that the path is represented by a string with keys or indices separated by the ‘|’ character.

Parameters:

  • collection (scToolkit.experiment_dict, dict, list or str) – The dictionary or list to search through. The str option is for internal use.

  • pattern (str) – The regular expression pattern to match against keys or values.

  • path (str, optional) – The current path in the dictionary or list, used during recursion to track the hierarchy. Defaults to “”.

  • sep (str, optional) – The delimiter used to split the string lists. Defaults to ‘”][”’.

  • start (str, optional) – The delimiter used to split the string lists. Defaults to ‘[”’.

  • end (str, optional) – The delimiter used to split the string lists. Defaults to ‘”]’.

Returns:

This function does not return a value. Instead, it prints out the paths of matched keys or values.

Return type:

None

Raises:

None – This function does not raise any exceptions explicitly.

Calls:

search_regex_nested_dict_list

Called By:

search_regex_nested_dict_list, search_scToolkit_function_legacy

Tags:

utils

scToolkit.sc_config.search_scToolkit_function_legacy(regex: str) → None

Searches for a given regular expression pattern within a nested dictionary list structure generated by create_code_structure() and performs a regex search.

This function is designed to help locate specific patterns within a potentially large and complex nested structure, which is commonly found in Scanpy’s API documentation or similar resources.

Note

If the interpreter tells you the function is not available, use search_scToolkit_function_legacy(“function_name”) and it will show you the list of posible available modules having this function. just change the old_module.function to new_module.function and it should do the trick ;)

Parameters:

regex (str) – A regular expression pattern to search within the nested dictionary list structure.

Returns:

This function performs the search operation but does not return any value.

Return type:

None

Raises:

  • ValueError – If the provided regex is not a valid regular expression pattern.

  • TypeError – If the input is not a string.

Calls:

create_code_structure, search_regex_nested_dict_list

Tags:

codebase

scToolkit.sc_config.update_key_in_config(config: dict, key_to_update: str, new_value: object) → None

Recursively update all keys named ‘key_to_update’ in a nested config dictionary if their type matches the type of ‘new_value’ (or they are None).

Parameters:

  • config (dict) – The configuration dictionary to modify in-place.

  • key_to_update (str) – The key name to update.

  • new_value (object) – The new value to assign.

Returns:

None

Calls:

update_key_in_config

Called By:

update_key_in_config

Tags:

config, utils

scToolkit.sc_config.update_nested(base: dict | list, updates: dict | list) → None

Recursively updates a nested dictionary or list structure with values from another dictionary or list.

This function modifies the base structure in place by merging values from updates. If both base and updates contain dictionaries, they are recursively merged. If they contain lists, corresponding elements are updated where possible. If the index in updates exceeds the length of base, the new elements are appended.

Note

  • The function modifies the base object in place and does not return a new structure.

  • If updates contains new keys or indices, they are added to base.

  • Lists are updated by modifying their corresponding elements or appending new ones.

Parameters:

  • base (dict | list) – The original dictionary or list to be updated.

  • updates (dict | list) – The dictionary or list containing updates.

Returns:

None

Raises:

TypeError – If either base or updates is not a dictionary or a list.

Calls:

update_nested

Called By:

update_nested

Tags:

utils

scToolkit.sc_config.update_nested_dict(nested_dict: dict, nested_dict_update: dict) → dict

Updates a nested dictionary with values from another nested dictionary.

This function recursively updates a nested dictionary nested_dict with the values from another nested dictionary nested_dict_update. If the same key exists in both dictionaries and the value associated with that key is also a dictionary, it will merge the dictionaries rather than overwriting. This allows for deep merging of nested dictionaries.

Note

This function is similar to dict.update(other_dict), but it supports nested dictionaries.

Example use case:

Consider a scenario where you have a default configuration dictionary and you want to update it with user-specified overrides, while preserving the nested structure.

Parameters:

  • nested_dict (dict) – The dictionary to update. This is the dictionary that will be modified.

  • nested_dict_update (dict) – The dictionary where the update comes from. This contains the new values that will be merged into nested_dict.

Returns:

The updated dictionary with the merged values from nested_dict_update.

Return type:

dict

References

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3232943/update-value-of-a-nested-dictionary-of-varying-depth

Calls:

update_nested_dict

Called By:

load_h5ad_old, update_nested_dict

Tags:

utils

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

sc_plots

  • `sc_plots` — source Unified plotting interface for UMAPs, dot plots, violins, heatmaps, ridline, density. Designed for consistency, with shared color handling and auto-labeling across all major marker and enrichment visualizations.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════

This module provides a comprehensive suite of visualization and analysis tools for single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data, leveraging the adata structure for organizing and managing the data. It includes functions to visualize quality control (QC), clustering results, and differentially expressed genes (DEGs) across clusters and conditions.

Key functionalities include:

  1. Visualization of DEGs and Clustering: - plot_per_group_DEG_dotplot_n_gene_dendrogram: Generates dot plots and dendrograms for DEGs per cluster. - plot_per_group_stacked_violins: Produces stacked violin plots for visualizing gene expression. - plot_per_group_DEG_umaps: Creates UMAP visualizations for DEGs per cluster. - get_DEGs_per_group_for_plotting: Retrieves DEGs for plotting based on various filtering criteria. - create_gene_dendrogram: Generates and saves a dendrogram for specified genes.

  2. Quality Control and Summary Visualizations: - plot_hist_of_each_layer: Displays histograms of each layer in an adata object. - plot_qc_violins: Generates violin plots for quality control metrics. - plot_qc_scatter: Creates scatter plots for QC metrics. - plot_qc_scatter_combinations: Generates scatter plots for combinations of QC metrics. - plot_filtering: Visualizes QC steps with violins and scatterplots. - show_num_cells_and_count_per_gene: Logs the number of cells and counts per gene.

  3. Specialized Plotting: - plot_ridgeline: Creates a ridgeline plot for visualizing overlapping data distributions. - plot_density_scatter: Generates density scatter plots for cell counts vs. genes. - plot_ref_dotplots: Creates dot plots for marker genes per cluster. - plot_ref_stacked_violins: Generates stacked violin plots for marker genes. - plot_mediods_heatmap: Creates a heatmap for gene expression across clusters.

  4. UMAP Plot Helpers: - discrete_umap_helper: Creates a discretized UMAP plot for specific markers. - continuos_umap_helper: Generates continuous UMAP plots for visualizing gradients of marker genes. - plot_umap: Wrapper function for creating UMAP plots with custom configurations.

Additional Details:

  • The module is designed for use with the adata object from the scanpy library, and many functions rely on precomputed data within this object.

  • Funcations often provide options for customizing the output, such as specifying clusters, adjusting plot aesthetics, and saving figures to specified directories.

  • Dependencies include scanpy, matplotlib, and other common Python libraries for data manipulation and visualization.

This module is intended for use in exploratory data analysis and publication-quality visualizations of single-cell RNA-seq data.

scToolkit.sc_plots.calc_dotplot_figsize(shape: tuple[int, int], xlabels: list[str], ylabels: list[str], font_size: float = 10, dpi: int = 100, dot_spacing: float = 0.85, dot_unit: float = 0.25, legend_stack: int = 2, legend_box_pad: float = 1.2, title_height: float = 0.4) → tuple[float, float]

Compute a content-aware figure size (in inches) for a Marsilea split-dotplot.

All inputs are interpreted as physical constraints and rendered elements. The function ensures that the largest dot fits cleanly into the spacing and that labels and legends are properly padded.

This considers:

  • Dot matrix size based on dot count and spacing.

  • Max circle radius for visual margin.

  • Label size from longest x and y labels.

  • Legend box size from estimated text width/height.

  • Top padding for an optional title.

Parameters:

  • shape – (rows, cols) of the dotplot grid.

  • xlabels – List of bottom axis labels.

  • ylabels – List of left axis labels.

  • font_size – Font size in points used for labels and legends.

  • dpi – Resolution in dots per inch. Controls pt to inch conversion.

  • dot_spacing – Distance (in “dot units”) between dot centers.

  • dot_unit – Physical size (in inches) of one spacing unit.

  • legend_stack – Number of legend rows.

  • legend_box_pad – Vertical padding multiplier for legends.

  • title_height – Vertical padding (in inches) to reserve for title.

Returns:

Tuple of (width, height) in inches to pass to matplotlib or Marsilea board.

Return type:

tuple[float, float]

Called By:

plot_dotplot, plot_proximap, plot_split_dotplot

scToolkit.sc_plots.calc_hierarchical_order(arrays: list[ndarray], axis: int = 0, substitute: float | None = 0.0, return_linkage: bool = False, **kwargs) → list[int] | tuple[list[int], linkage]

Computes a dendrogram-based ordering of either rows or columns across one or more matrices.

Parameters:

  • arrays (list[np.ndarray]) – List of 2D arrays to combine for clustering.

  • axis (int, optional) – 0 = cluster rows, 1 = cluster columns (default: 0). Defaults to 0.

  • substitute (float | None, optional) – Value to replace NaNs. None = no substitution. Defaults to 0.0.

  • return_linkage (bool, optional) – If True, also return the linkate Defaults to False.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments passed to scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage.

Returns:

  • list[int]: If not return_linkage, Ordered indices from hierarchical clustering.

  • tuple[list[int], linkage]: If return_linkage, Ordered indices from hierarchical clustering and Linkage

Return type:

list[int] | tuple[list[int], linkage]

Called By:

plot_dotplot, plot_split_dotplot

scToolkit.sc_plots.check_scoring_validity(adata: AnnData, key: str = 'n_unique') → None

Check and visualize the unique counts within the given adata object.

Note

  • This is for developing a scoring system for the n_unique, but possibly usable for any other scoring.

  • The keys “n_counts”, “n_genes” “pct_MT” must be present in the obs!

  • Automatically uses “{key}_score” and “{key}_score_norm” if present.

This function processes the adata object adata to visualize the distribution of unique counts across different attributes of cells and genes. The function creates histograms for various metrics such as ‘n_unique_score’ and ‘n_unique_score_norm’, both for obs and var in the adata object. Additionally, it generates scatter plots to explore the relationships between these metrics and other features like ‘n_counts’, ‘pct_MT’, and ‘n_genes’.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • key (str, optional) – A obs key to check. Defaults to “n_unique”.

Returns:

The function creates and displays plots, but does not return any

value.

Return type:

None

Calls:

get_n_unique

Tags:

QC, config, obs, var, visualization

scToolkit.sc_plots.continuos_umap_helper(adata: AnnData, keys: list[str], part: str, with_figure_explaination: bool, name: str = '', highlighers: str = '################################################################################', config: dict | None = None) → None

Helper function to create a continuous UMAP plot for specified keys.

This function generates and saves a UMAP plot visualizing the gradients of specified marker genes across cells in the adata. It can optionally log a textual explanation of the figure being saved and supports customization via a configuration dictionary.

Note

This is more a helper for run_downstream.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • keys (list[str]) – List of marker genes to be visualized on the UMAP.

  • part (str) – Directory part of the analysis where this should be saved.

  • with_figure_explaination (bool) – If True, logs an explanation of the figure being saved.

  • name (str, optional) – Name to be included in the saved file name. Defaults to “”.

  • highlighers (str, optional) – Highlighter string for visual emphasis in logs. Defaults to 80 “#” characters. Defaults to “#” * 80.

  • config (dict | None, optional) – Configuration dictionary. uns[“config”]`` if not provided. Defaults to None.

Returns:

The function directly generates and saves a UMAP plot.

Return type:

None

Calls:

get_colormap, get_save_path, plot_umap

Called By:

plot_per_group_DEG_umaps, run_downstream

scToolkit.sc_plots.create_gene_dendrogram(adata: AnnData, geneset: list[str], fig_name: str, top_n_genes: str = '', figsize: Sequence[float | int] = (10, 5), part: str = '/downstream/') → None

Create and save a dendrogram plot for a given set of genes.

Generates a dendrogram using hierarchical clustering for a specified gene set in the adata object and saves the resulting plot as a PDF. The dendrogram is saved with the provided figure name and optional suffix.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • geneset (list[str]) – List of gene names to include in the dendrogram.

  • fig_name (str) – Base name of the output figure.

  • top_n_genes (str, optional) – Optional suffix for the filename to indicate top N genes. Defaults to “”.

  • figsize (Sequence[float | int], optional) – Width and height of the output figure in inches.

  • part (str, optional) – Path to the directory where the figure will be saved.

Returns:

None

Raises:

FileNotFoundError – If the specified output directory does not exist.

Called By:

plot_per_group_DEG_dotplot_n_gene_dendrogram

Tags:

DEG, clustering, io, var, visualization

scToolkit.sc_plots.discrete_umap_helper(adata: AnnData, keys: list[str], part: str, with_figure_explaination: bool, name: str = '', highlighers: str = '################################################################################', config: dict | None = None) → None

Helper function to create a discretized UMAP plot for specified keys.

This function generates a UMAP plot for a set of marker genes within an adata object, with the option to log an explanation of the figure being saved. The function allows for a custom name in the filename and configuration options for the UMAP plotting parameters.

Note

This is more a helper for run_downstream.

Parameters:

  • adata (anndata.AnnData) – Adata object.

  • keys (list[str]) – List of marker genes to be visualized on the UMAP.

  • part (str) – Directory part of the analysis where this should be saved.

  • with_figure_explaination (bool) – If True, logs an explanation of the figure being saved.

  • name (str, optional) – Name to be included in the saved file name. Defaults to “”.
